raw_response: {"rating": -2, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial that argues in favor of maintaining and building on Common Core–aligned standards and rebuts a conservative critic’s claims. The author’s own prose frames repeal as harmful and emphasizes states’ continued refinement of the standards, yielding a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author directly counters the critic with an assertive claim that ESSA ensures state and local control, positioning the critic as wrong (“Contrary to Stotsky’s claim…”); (2) Approving language toward Common Core appears in the author’s voice: states are “overwhelmingly” reviewing/refining and “build on them even further—exactly as they were designed”; (3) The author uses normative judgments against repeal (“ill-advised course of action,” leading to “inferior standards”), signaling a clear stance against a largely right-leaning anti–Common Core movement; (4) The headline endorses a pro–Common Core trajectory (“Build on Common Core Framework”), priming a favorable view; (5) Source selection is one-sided: the piece undermines the lone opposing figure with an authorial aside about Texas never adopting Common Core, while platforming supportive analyses and voices, without balancing counterarguments."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that argues in favor of maintaining and building on Common Core–aligned standards and rebuts a conservative critic’s claims. The author’s own prose frames repeal as harmful and emphasizes states’ continued refinement of the standards, yielding a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author directly counters the critic with an assertive claim that ESSA ensures state and local control, positioning the critic as wrong (“Contrary to Stotsky’s claim…”); (2) Approving language toward Common Core appears in the author’s voice: states are “overwhelmingly” reviewing/refining and “build on them even further—exactly as they were designed”; (3) The author uses normative judgments against repeal (“ill-advised course of action,” leading to “inferior standards”), signaling a clear stance against a largely right-leaning anti–Common Core movement; (4) The headline endorses a pro–Common Core trajectory (“Build on Common Core Framework”), priming a favorable view; (5) Source selection is one-sided: the piece undermines the lone opposing figure with an authorial aside about Texas never adopting Common Core, while platforming supportive analyses and voices, without balancing counterarguments.

explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that argues in favor of maintaining and building on Common Core–aligned standards and rebuts a conservative critic’s claims. The author’s own prose frames repeal as harmful and emphasizes states’ continued refinement of the standards, yielding a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author directly counters the critic with an assertive claim that ESSA ensures state and local control, positioning the critic as wrong (“Contrary to Stotsky’s claim…”); (2) Approving language toward Common Core appears in the author’s voice: states are “overwhelmingly” reviewing/refining and “build on them even further—exactly as they were designed”; (3) The author uses normative judgments against repeal (“ill-advised course of action,” leading to “inferior standards”), signaling a clear stance against a largely right-leaning anti–Common Core movement; (4) The headline endorses a pro–Common Core trajectory (“Build on Common Core Framework”), priming a favorable view; (5) Source selection is one-sided: the piece undermines the lone opposing figure with an authorial aside about Texas never adopting Common Core, while platforming supportive analyses and voices, without balancing counterarguments.
